display_name: Whether hosts use SLAAC or DHCPv6 is decided by the RA M/O flags — an idle DHCPv6 server is not necessarily broken
context: Hosts are not getting addresses from the DHCPv6 server (or are getting SLAAC addresses when DHCPv6 was intended), while the DHCPv6 server itself appears healthy
observation: DHCPv6 server shows little or no Solicit traffic, and hosts have SLAAC addresses; or hosts wait without any global address
naive_interpretation: The DHCPv6 server, relay, or scope is broken
correct_interpretation: Host address-source selection in IPv6 is driven entirely by two flags in the Router Advertisement, not by the DHCPv6 server. The M (Managed) flag tells hosts to obtain addresses via stateful DHCPv6; the O (Other) flag tells them to obtain other parameters (DNS, etc.) via stateless DHCPv6. If M=0, hosts will SLAAC and never even send a Solicit — so a perfectly healthy DHCPv6 server sits idle, which is not a server fault. Conversely, if M=1 but no working DHCPv6 server/relay answers, hosts wait and get no global address. Additionally, the prefix's Autonomous (A) flag controls whether hosts SLAAC from that prefix at all. Before debugging the DHCPv6 server, read the RA flags on the segment and confirm they match the intended addressing model; fix the flags on the router, not the server.
why: RFC 4861 RA M/O flags select stateful vs stateless configuration; hosts that see M=0 never initiate DHCPv6
The DHCPv6 server cannot make hosts use it — only the RA can — so 'no Solicits' often means a flag problem, not a server problem
The prefix Autonomous (A) flag independently controls SLAAC, so addressing can split between SLAAC and DHCPv6 depending on flag combinations

confirmation_commands.purpose: Read the advertised Managed/Other config flags and prefix Autonomous flag
Check 'managed-config-flag'/'other-config-flag' and how hosts are told to configure
Inspect advertised M/O flags and prefix flags
Infer whether hosts followed SLAAC (prefix route) or waited on DHCPv6

traps: Debugging the DHCPv6 server when M=0 means hosts never ask it
Setting M=1 without a reachable DHCPv6 server/relay, leaving hosts with no global address
Forgetting the prefix A flag — hosts can SLAAC even when DHCPv6 addressing was intended
Inconsistent M/O flags across redundant routers on the same segment producing mixed behavior

sources.title: RFC 4861 — Neighbor Discovery for IPv6 (neighbor cache states 7.3.2)
